name: Netflix
overview:
Netflix is a video streaming service that delivers high-quality video to 200M+ subscribers worldwide. The
system must handle massive video upload and transcoding (content ingestion), serve video streams with
adaptive bitrate to handle varying network conditions, provide personalized content recommendations, and
maintain high availability (99.99% uptime). Video delivery is the dominant workload, accounting for ~15% of
global internet traffic.

inScope: Stream video with adaptive bitrate (360p to 4K)
Content ingestion: upload, transcode, and publish new titles
Resume playback from where user left off

items: Video start time <2 seconds; buffering rate <0.1%
99.99% availability — streaming outages are high-visibility incidents
Adaptive bitrate: gracefully degrade quality on poor networks rather than buffering
CDN cache hit rate >99% to protect origin infrastructure
Recommendation latency <200ms; model retrained daily on fresh engagement data

entities.name: Video
entities.schema:
{
  video_id: UUID,
  title: string,
  description: string,

  duration_seconds: int,
  genres: [string],
  cast: [string],
  release_year: int,
  content_rating: string,
  manifest_url: string  // HLS/DASH manifest
}

entities.name: VideoChunk
entities.schema:
{
  video_id: UUID,
  quality: enum(360P|480P|720P|1080P|4K),
  chunk_index: int,
  duration_seconds: float,
  s3_key: string,
  size_bytes: int
}

entities.name: WatchHistory
entities.schema:
{
  user_id: string,
  video_id: UUID,
  watched_at: timestamp,
  progress_seconds: int,
  completed: boolean
}

approaches.title: Single Quality Stream from Origin
approaches.description: Serve a single video quality from the origin server. Users on slow connections get buffering; users on fast connections get poor quality. No adaptation to network conditions.
approaches.tradeoff: Poor user experience on variable networks; no quality adaptation.

approaches.title: CDN with Fixed Quality Levels
approaches.description: Pre-transcode video to 5 quality levels; serve from CDN. Client selects quality manually. CDN reduces origin load. But manual quality selection is poor UX; client can't adapt to changing network conditions.
approaches.tradeoff: No automatic adaptation; manual quality selection is poor UX.

approaches.title: Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (HLS/DASH) via CDN
approaches.description: Video is transcoded into multiple quality levels (360p to 4K) and segmented into 4-second chunks. An HLS/DASH manifest lists all quality variants. The client player monitors download speed and buffer level, automatically switching quality levels between chunks. Netflix's Open Connect CDN deploys appliances in ISP data centers, serving content from within the ISP network for minimal latency.
approaches.tradeoff: Transcoding pipeline is complex and compute-intensive; manifest management adds

approaches.title: overhead.
approaches.description: Recommendations Popularity-Based Recommend the most-watched content globally. No personalization. Users see the same recommendations regardless of their taste.
approaches.tradeoff: No personalization; poor for niche content discovery.

approaches.title: Collaborative Filtering
approaches.description: Recommend content that similar users watched. 'Users who watched X also watched Y.' Better personalization but cold-start problem for new users and new content.
approaches.tradeoff: Cold-start problem; doesn't use content features (genre, cast).

approaches.title: Two-Tower Neural Network
approaches.description: A two-tower model: one tower encodes the user (watch history, ratings, demographics), another encodes the video (genre, cast, description embeddings). Dot product of towers gives relevance score. Pre-compute video embeddings offline. At query time, compute user embedding and find nearest video embeddings using approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search (Faiss). Recommendations are pre-computed daily for all users and stored in Redis.
approaches.tradeoff: Model training requires significant compute; ANN index must be rebuilt when new content is

components.name: Pipeline
components.responsibility: Accepts raw video uploads; transcodes to multiple qualities; generates

components.name: Open Connect CDN
components.responsibility: Netflix's proprietary CDN; ISP-embedded appliances; serves video chunks

components.name: Video API
components.responsibility: Serves manifests and metadata; handles playback session management

components.name: Recommendation Service
components.responsibility: Serves pre-computed recommendations from Redis; falls back to ANN search

components.name: Watch History Service
components.responsibility: Records playback progress; enables resume; feeds recommendation model

components.name: S3 (Origin)
components.responsibility: Stores all transcoded video chunks and manifests; CDN origin

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Streams watch events for recommendation model training

components.name: Cassandra
components.responsibility: Stores watch history; partition key = user_id; clustering key = watched_at

components.name: Redis
components.responsibility: Caches pre-computed recommendations and video metadata

questions.title: 2. Adaptive Bitrate Algorithm
questions.content:
Netflix's client player uses a buffer-based ABR algorithm (BOLA). The algorithm maintains a target buffer level
(e.g., 30 seconds). If the buffer is above target, it requests higher quality chunks. If below target, it requests
lower quality. The algorithm also considers the download speed of the last chunk to predict future bandwidth.
This gives smooth quality transitions without frequent oscillations.

questions.title: 3. Open Connect CDN
questions.content:
Netflix's Open Connect CDN places appliances (servers with 100TB+ of storage) directly in ISP data centers.
Popular content is pre-positioned on these appliances. When a user requests a video, the CDN routes them to
the nearest appliance. If the appliance doesn't have the content, it fetches from S3 origin and caches it. This
reduces Netflix's transit costs and improves latency for users.

questions.title: 4. Chaos Engineering
questions.content:
Netflix pioneered chaos engineering with Chaos Monkey, which randomly terminates production instances to
test resilience. The system is designed to handle failures gracefully: if a CDN node fails, the client retries with a
different node. If the recommendation service is slow, the API returns cached recommendations. If Cassandra
is unavailable, watch history writes are queued in Kafka for later processing.

questions.title: 5. A/B Testing at Scale
questions.content:
Netflix runs hundreds of A/B tests simultaneously. Each user is assigned to a test cell (a combination of
experiment variants). The assignment is stored in a user profile service and used to serve different UI layouts,
recommendation algorithms, and encoding parameters. Results are analyzed using a proprietary
experimentation platform that accounts for network effects (users in the same household may influence each
other).

keyFlows: ① Client requests manifest → Video API → CDN (Open Connect appliance)
② CDN serves HLS chunks; ABR player adapts quality to bandwidth
③ CDN miss → fetch from S3 origin → cache on CDN node
④ Watch events → Kafka → Cassandra (history) + Redis (rec update)
⑤ Recommendation Svc: pre-computed recs from Redis (two-tower model)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study HLS (HTTP Live Streaming) and DASH (Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP):
how manifests work, the difference between segment-based and chunk-based streaming, and how ABR
algorithms work.
Expert Note: Learn about Netflix's two-tower recommendation model: how user and item embeddings are
trained, how approximate nearest neighbor search (Faiss/ScaNN) enables fast retrieval.
Expert Note: Understand Netflix's Open Connect CDN: how ISP peering works, how content is
pre-positioned using popularity data, and how the CDN routing algorithm selects the best appliance.

Expert Note: Study chaos engineering principles: the difference between chaos experiments and fault
injection, how to design experiments that test specific failure modes without causing outages.
Expert Note: Learn about video codec comparison: H.264 vs. H.265 vs. AV1 compression efficiency, the
trade-off between encoding time and compression ratio, and why Netflix uses per-title encoding.
